flashcards.q: What is reflection of light?
flashcards.a: The bouncing back of light rays into the same medium when they strike a polished or shiny surface.

flashcards.q: State the two laws of reflection.
flashcards.a: (1) Angle of incidence = Angle of reflection; (2) Incident ray, reflected ray, and normal at the point of incidence all lie in the same plane.

flashcards.q: What is a spherical mirror?
flashcards.a: A mirror whose reflecting surface forms part of the surface of a hollow sphere.

flashcards.q: Define the 'Pole' (P) of a spherical mirror.
flashcards.a: The geometric centre of the reflecting surface of the mirror.

flashcards.q: What is the 'Centre of Curvature' (C) of a spherical mirror?
flashcards.a: The centre of the sphere of which the reflecting surface of the mirror forms a part.

flashcards.q: Define the 'Principal Axis' of a spherical mirror.
flashcards.a: The straight line passing through the pole and the centre of curvature of the mirror.

flashcards.q: What is the 'Aperture' of a spherical mirror?
flashcards.a: The diameter of the circular outline of the reflecting surface.

flashcards.q: Define the 'Principal Focus' (F) of a concave mirror.
flashcards.a: The point on the principal axis where rays parallel to the axis actually meet after reflection.

flashcards.q: Define 'Focal Length' (f) of a spherical mirror.
flashcards.a: The distance between the pole and the principal focus of the mirror.

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between Radius of Curvature (R) and Focal Length (f)?
flashcards.a: R = 2f (For mirrors with small apertures).

flashcards.q: List two common uses of concave mirrors.
flashcards.a: Torch reflectors, vehicle headlights, shaving mirrors, and dentist mirrors.

flashcards.q: Why are convex mirrors used as rear-view mirrors in vehicles?
flashcards.a: They always give an erect, diminished image and provide a wider field of view.

flashcards.q: What does a negative sign in magnification (m) signify?
flashcards.a: The image is real and inverted.

flashcards.q: What does a positive sign in magnification (m) signify?
flashcards.a: The image is virtual and erect.

flashcards.q: State the Mirror Formula.
Define Refraction of light.
flashcards.a: The bending of light as it passes obliquely from one transparent medium to another due to a change in speed.

flashcards.q: What is Snell's Law of refraction?
flashcards.a: The ratio of the sine of the angle of incidence to the sine of the angle of refraction is a constant (sin i / sin r = n).

flashcards.q: What is the 'Refractive Index'?
flashcards.a: The ratio of the speed of light in vacuum (or medium 1) to its speed in a given medium (or medium 2).

flashcards.q: What is the absolute refractive index of a medium?
flashcards.a: n_m = c / v (where c is speed of light in vacuum and v is speed in the medium).

flashcards.q: Define 'Optical Density'.
flashcards.a: The measure of a medium's ability to refract light; higher refractive index means higher optical density.

flashcards.q: What is a lens?
flashcards.a: A transparent material bound by two surfaces, at least one of which is spherical.

flashcards.q: Define a 'Convex Lens'.
flashcards.a: A lens thicker at the middle than at the edges; also called a converging lens.

flashcards.q: Define a 'Concave Lens'.
flashcards.a: A lens thinner at the middle than at the edges; also called a diverging lens.

flashcards.q: Define 'Optical Centre' (O) of a lens.
flashcards.a: The central point of the lens through which light passes without any deviation.

flashcards.q: State the Lens Formula.
What is the 'Power' of a lens?
flashcards.a: The degree of convergence or divergence of light rays; it is the reciprocal of the focal length (P = 1/f).

flashcards.q: Name the SI unit of power of a lens.
flashcards.a: Dioptre (D).

flashcards.q: Define 1 Dioptre.
flashcards.a: The power of a lens whose focal length is exactly 1 metre.

flashcards.q: What is the sign of power for a convex lens?
flashcards.a: Positive (+).

flashcards.q: What is the sign of power for a concave lens?
flashcards.a: Negative (-).

flashcards.q: What happens to the speed of light when it enters an optically denser medium?
flashcards.a: Its speed decreases.

flashcards.q: How is net power (P) calculated for lenses in contact?
flashcards.a: By taking the algebraic sum of individual powers: P = P1 + P2 + P3 + ...
